slides.title: MQTT Masterclass
slides.subtitle: From IoT Fundamentals to AI-Enhanced Edge Computing

slides.title: Introduction to MQTT & Pub-Sub
slides.subtitle: Understanding the lightweight communication paradigm for the Internet of Things

list.text: Lightweight Transport: Message Queuing Telemetry Transport is a publish-subscribe network protocol designed for resource-constrained devices.
Low Bandwidth: Minimizes network overhead with small header sizes (starting at just 2 bytes), making it ideal for satellite or cellular connections.
High Latency Tolerant: Built to withstand unstable, intermittent, and low-quality network connections common in remote deployments.
1999 - Oil Pipeline Monitoring: Created by Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM) and Arlen Nipper (Eurotech) to monitor oil pipelines over expensive satellite links.
2014 - OASIS MQTT v3.1.1: Formally standardized by the OASIS consortium, becoming the industry standard for IoT systems.
2019 - MQTT v5.0 Released: Introduces crucial enterprise features like User Properties, Shared Subscriptions, and improved error handling.

slides.title: Request-Response vs Pub-Sub
leftPoints: Tightly coupled: Client must know server's address and status.
Unidirectional: Server cannot push messages to client spontaneously.
High overhead: Large HTTP headers (hundreds of bytes) and verbose payload formats.
rightPoints: Decoupled: Clients communicate through a broker without knowing each other.
Bidirectional: Real-time asynchronous pushes supported natively.
Low overhead: Binary protocol headers are extremely small (minimum 2 bytes).

list.text: Time Decoupling: Publishers and subscribers do not need to be active at the same time to exchange messages.
Space Decoupling: Neither publisher nor subscriber needs to know the IP address or port of the other.
Thread Decoupling: Publishing and receiving operations do not block execution threads synchronously.
TCP/IP Foundation: Relies on the connection-oriented TCP protocol to ensure ordered, lossless byte streams.
Binary Protocol: Fields are packed at the byte/bit level rather than text (unlike HTTP, WebSocket, or gRPC JSON).
Payload Agnostic: The message payload is a raw byte array; it can carry JSON, XML, Protobuf, or binary files.

slides.title: Core Concepts & Lifecycle
slides.subtitle: Understanding connections, subscriptions, and message flow

list.text: Client: Any device (microcontroller, server, phone) that connects to the broker to publish or subscribe to topics.
Broker: The central router. Receives publishes, filters messages by topic, and dispatches them to matching subscribers.
No Store-and-Forward (by default): Brokers route messages immediately; they do not store historic data unless configured.

box.title: CONNECT Control Packet Structure
box.code:
Client                     Broker
  |                          |
  |--- CONNECT ------------->|  - Protocol Name & Level
  |                          |  - Client Identifier (ClientId)
  |                          |  - Keep Alive interval
  |                          |  - CleanSession / CleanStart flag
  |                          |  - Username / Password (Optional)
  |                          |  - Last Will Message (Optional)
  |<-- CONNACK --------------|  - Session Present Flag
  |                          |  - Connect Reason Code

list.text: Session Present: Indicates if the broker has saved state (subscriptions, queued messages) from a previous session.
Reason Code 0x00: Connection accepted and established successfully.
Error Reason Codes: Indicates failures like unauthorized (0x87), client identifier not valid (0x85), or server unavailable (0x88).
Topic Name: The hierarchical string classifying the message channel (e.g., 'factory/zone1/temp').
Payload: The actual data. The broker does not read, modify, or translate this byte array.
Packet Identifier: Unique 16-bit integer used to track acknowledgments in QoS 1 and QoS 2 flows.

box.title: SUBSCRIBE and SUBACK Packets
box.code:
Client                     Broker
  |                          |
  |--- SUBSCRIBE ----------->|  - Packet Identifier (e.g., 0x0001)
  |                            - Topic Filters (e.g., 'sensor/+/temp')
  |                            - Requested QoS Level (0, 1, or 2)
  |                          |
  |<-- SUBACK ---------------|  - Packet Identifier match (0x0001)
  |                          |  - List of Granted QoS Levels / Reason Codes

list.text: Keep Alive Interval: Client-defined timeout in seconds (e.g. 60s). Max time allowed between control packets.
PINGREQ: Sent by client if no other packet has been transmitted within the Keep Alive window.
PINGRESP: Sent by broker immediately upon receiving PINGREQ. If not received, client terminates the TCP connection.
Clean Session (true): Broker discards any previous state on disconnect, and deletes the session state on reconnect.
Clean Session (false): Broker retains client subscriptions and queues outstanding QoS 1/2 messages while the client is offline.
Resuming Session: On reconnect, client receives all queued messages that were published during its downtime.

slides.title: Quality of Service (QoS) Levels
slides.subtitle: Fine-tuning reliability and network performance in MQTT

list.text: Fire and Forget: Message is sent once without any acknowledgment handshake. No retry logic is executed.
Lowest Overhead: Consumes the minimum network bandwidth and requires zero client/broker storage.
Use Cases: High-frequency, non-critical telemetry data where single drops do not matter (e.g. real-time CPU utilization).

box.title: Acknowledged Delivery & Retries
box.code:
Sender                     Receiver
  |                          |
  |--- PUBLISH (PacketId=1)->|  - Store message locally
  |                          |  - Process message
  |<-- PUBACK (PacketId=1)---|  - Send acknowledgment
  |                          |
  |-- (If PUBACK lost) ------|
  |--- PUBLISH (PacketId=1)->|  - Redelivered (duplicate flag set)

box.title: Four-Way Handshake Protocol
box.code:
Sender                     Receiver
  |                          |
  |--- PUBLISH (PacketId=2)->|  - Store packet ID, block duplicate processing
  |<-- PUBREC (PacketId=2)---|  - Publish Received (Received flag set)
  |--- PUBREL (PacketId=2)-->|  - Publish Release (Sender releases state)
  |<-- PUBCOMP (PacketId=2)--|  - Publish Complete (Handshake completed)

slides.title: Comparing QoS Levels
leftPoints: QoS 0: Fast, unbuffered, data loss is possible during network disruptions.
QoS 1: Guarantees delivery, but duplicate messages may occur if ACK is lost.
Resource usage: Low memory and compute requirements; standard network load.
rightPoints: Guaranteed once: Prevents duplicates entirely, ensuring exact count consistency.
Latency cost: Requires 4 control messages, raising latency and network traffic.
Storage cost: Sender and receiver must track packet state during handshake.

list.text: Persistent State: Only applies if the client connected with CleanSession=false (or SessionExpiry > 0 in v5.0).
QoS 1 & 2 Only: The broker queues only QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages. QoS 0 messages are discarded instantly if the client is offline.
Queue Limits: High-performance brokers have memory limits. Excess queued messages trigger drops or overwrite oldest items.

slides.title: Topic Wildcards & Hierarchy Design
slides.subtitle: Designing structured, efficient, and secure topic trees

list.text: Topic Levels: Separated by forward slashes (e.g. 'home/livingroom/temperature'). Levels represent logical domains.
Case Sensitivity: Topics are case-sensitive. 'Home/temp' and 'home/temp' are distinct channels.
Leading Slashes: Avoid starting topics with a slash (e.g. '/home/temp'). This creates an empty leading level, adding overhead.
Pattern: The '+' character matches exactly one topic level anywhere in the topic string.
Example: Subscribing to 'sensors/+/temperature' matches 'sensors/livingroom/temperature' and 'sensors/kitchen/temperature'.
No Match: It does NOT match 'sensors/livingroom/north/temperature' (requires matching two levels).
Pattern: The '#' character matches zero or more trailing levels in the topic hierarchy.
Positioning: Must always be the last character in the topic filter, preceded by a forward slash.
Example: 'home/livingroom/#' matches 'home/livingroom', 'home/livingroom/temp', and 'home/livingroom/lights/status'.

box.title: Comparing Filter Matches
box.code:
Topic Filter                 | Target Topic                  | Result
-----------------------------+-------------------------------+-------
home/+/temp                  | home/kitchen/temp             | MATCH
home/+/temp                  | home/kitchen/sensor/temp      | FAIL
home/+/temp                  | home/temp                     | FAIL
home/#                       | home                          | MATCH
home/#                       | home/kitchen/fridge/humidity  | MATCH
sensors/+/+/status           | sensors/zone1/node12/status   | MATCH

list.text: Embed Client IDs: Include unique client or device IDs in the path (e.g. 'devices/client123/telemetry') to simplify authorization.
Action-Oriented Nodes: Use active suffixes for commands (e.g. '.../set') and state reports (e.g. '.../state').
Avoid Spaces/Special Chars: Keep topic levels alphanumeric, avoiding spaces, backslashes, or control characters.
Reserved Prefix: Topics starting with '$' are reserved for broker-internal statistics and configurations.
Telemetry Data: Subscribing to '$SYS/broker/clients/connected' provides real-time counts of active connections.
Read-Only: Clients cannot publish to '$SYS/' topics; they are strictly managed by the broker runtime.
Integer Mapping: Replaces long string topic names with a simple 16-bit integer ID (e.g. 'devices/sensor-long-name/temp' -> 1).
Initial Send: The client publishes a message with both the full topic string and the Alias integer (e.g., 1).
Subsequent Sends: The client sends subsequent messages with ONLY the Alias integer, leaving the topic field empty.

slides.title: Advanced Features & MQTT v5.0
slides.subtitle: Unlocking enterprise scalability and modern control features

list.text: Cloud Scale Compatibility: Improved features for high-traffic Kubernetes clusters and large load-balanced deployments.
Robust Error Reporting: Replaces silent failures with descriptive reason codes on all acknowledgment packets.
Extensibility: Introduces user properties to allow custom header metadata without altering the core binary schema.
Key-Value Pairs: Allows appending custom UTF-8 string metadata arrays to any message header (similar to HTTP headers).
Distributed Tracing: Enable correlation IDs to trace messages across brokers, gateways, and backend services.
Routing Metadata: Attach custom routing keys or content-type info (e.g., 'content-type: application/json').
Will Registration: During connection (CONNECT), the client provides a pre-configured topic and message payload (the Will).
Trigger Condition: If the client disconnects ungracefully (e.g. power loss, network dropout), the broker publishes the Will message.
Graceful Disconnect: If the client sends a DISCONNECT packet, the broker discards the Will, recognizing a clean exit.
Session Expiry Interval: Defines how long a disconnected persistent session remains active on the broker before deletion.
Message Expiry Interval: Message-level TTL. If a queued message sits longer than this limit, the broker discards it.
Use Case: Prevents broker memory exhaustion from dead sensors that never reconnect to consume their queues.
Packet-Level Feedback: v5.0 adds reason codes to CONNACK, PUBACK, SUBACK, UNSUBACK, and DISCONNECT.
Security Feedback: Allows brokers to explicitly send 'Not Authorized' (0x87) rather than silently closing TCP links.
Administrative Disconnect: Broker can notify the client of administrative shutdown (0x98) or protocol errors (0x82).
Traditional Pub-Sub: If three clients subscribe to 'telemetry', all three receive every published message.
Shared Subscriptions: Using prefix '$share/group_name/telemetry' distributes messages round-robin among group members.
Horizontal Scaling: Enables standard microservice worker groups to process massive IoT streams without duplicates.
Receive Maximum: Clients and brokers declare the maximum number of concurrent inflight QoS 1/2 messages they accept.
Buffer Overrun Protection: Prevents a fast publisher from overwhelming a slow consumer by blocking sends until ACKs return.
Dynamic Throttling: Keeps client/broker resources stable under spikes without drop-offs.

slides.title: MQTT Security & Hardening
slides.subtitle: Securing message transport, client identities, and access control

list.text: Encryption: Encrypts all payload and control packets, preventing eavesdropping and packet injection.
Standard Ports: Port 8883 is reserved for MQTT over TLS, while Port 1883 is used for unencrypted plain text.
Performance Overhead: TLS handshake adds latency and bytes, requiring hardware cryptographic accelerators on low-power chips.
Basic Auth: Username and Password sent inside the CONNECT packet. Should always be paired with TLS.
mTLS (Client Certificates): Dual-sided authentication where client validates broker, and broker validates client's X.509 certificate.
OAuth 2.0 / JWT: Passing dynamic web tokens in username/password fields for short-lived access authorization.
Granular Permissions: Restrict clients to specific read (subscribe) and write (publish) topic patterns.
Dynamic Rules: Use variables in ACL configurations (e.g. allow client '%c' to publish only to 'devices/%c/telemetry').
Isolation: Prevents compromised devices from sniffing data belonging to other sensors on the network.
Disable Anonymous: Ensure anonymous access is disabled in production settings (allow_anonymous false).
Rate Limiting: Configure connection rate limits and bandwidth throttling to mitigate denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.
Firewall Rules: Bind broker listener ports to localhost or private VPN adapters, exposing them only via reverse proxies.

slides.title: Authentication Trade-offs
leftPoints: Requires storage of password databases on brokers or directory sync (LDAP).
Vulnerable to credential harvesting if TLS is misconfigured.
rightPoints: Extremely secure; cryptographic validation without passwords over the air.
Requires Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) to issue and revoke certificates.
Heavy CPU computation overhead during handshakes on low-power devices.

slides.title: Broker Architecture & Clustering
slides.subtitle: Scaling MQTT broker fabrics to handle millions of concurrent IoT devices

list.text: Eclipse Mosquitto: Lightweight, single-threaded broker written in C. Ideal for low-resource environments (Raspberry Pi, edge gateways).
EMQX: Erlang-based high-performance broker. Supports massive concurrency, horizontal clustering, and rules engine.
HiveMQ: Java-based enterprise broker. Highly customizable with SDK plug-ins, designed for cloud-native orchestration.
Vertical Scaling: Increasing CPU cores, RAM, and tuning kernel socket parameters. Limited by single-system boundaries.
Horizontal Clustering: Distributing connections across multiple broker instances joined in a cluster.
State Sync: Requires clustering protocols to route messages to subscribers connected to different nodes.
Edge Link: A local broker (e.g. Mosquitto) is configured to connect as a client to a remote parent broker.
Route Mapping: Selected local topics are forwarded to the remote broker, and remote control topics are pulled back locally.
Store-and-Forward: If the WAN link fails, the edge broker queues telemetry messages locally and sends them once link recovers.
Shared Registry: Nodes must coordinate subscription records, knowing which client is connected to which node.
Message Forwarding: If client X publishes to Node A, and subscriber Y is on Node B, Node A forwards the message to Node B internally.
Distributed Lock Manager: Manages persistent sessions to ensure a client cannot hold active connections on two nodes simultaneously.
Sticky Sessions: Load balancer route clients with persistent sessions to their previous broker node if possible.
TCP Passthrough: Configure HAProxy/Nginx in layer 4 TCP mode to bypass HTTP proxy overhead, maximizing throughput.
Health Checks: Use custom broker health endpoints to immediately stop routing connections to failing nodes.

box.title: Optimizing Linux Kernel for Millions of Connections

list.text: File Descriptors: Increase maximum open files limits in system settings (e.g. fs.file-max) to allow over 100k TCP connections.
TCP Buffers: Tune read/write socket memory buffers (sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_rmem / wmem) to reduce RAM usage per socket.
TCP Keepalive: Lower system TCP keepalive time to quickly release dead sockets that went disconnected without DISCONNECT.

slides.title: Edge Computing & AIoT Integration
slides.subtitle: Combining real-time message streams with AI models at the network edge

list.text: Local Autonomy: Edge gateways run local brokers to allow sensors and switches to interact even when internet connectivity is down.
Data Filtering: Gateways consume high-frequency raw telemetry, aggregate it, and publish only processed trends to the cloud.
Low Latency: Crucial for closed-loop industrial controls where actions must take place in milliseconds.
Intelligence Shift: Moving machine learning execution from central cloud servers directly onto edge devices and gateways.
Telemetry Pipelines: MQTT acts as the real-time event pipeline feeding data into edge inference runtimes (e.g. TensorFlow Lite).
Model Outputs: Model predictions and classification events are published back to MQTT topics for downstream execution.
Continuous Monitoring: Sensors stream vibration, temperature, and current data via MQTT to local edge processors.
Anomaly Detection: Local AI models analyze real-time streams to identify deviations from normal operating patterns.
Alert Dispatching: If anomaly is detected, broker dispatches high-priority warning message to maintenance terminals.
Multimodal Inputs: AI agents subscribe to MQTT event feeds to understand current physical contexts.
Dynamic Actions: LLMs generate automation rules or MQTT commands dynamically in response to natural language goals.
Next-Gen Home: Future smart home systems using ambient models to adjust heating and lighting by parsing telemetry logs.
Topic Standardization: Sparkplug B defines strict topic structures (e.g., 'spBv1.0/group/message_type/edge_node') for plug-and-play setups.
Compressed Payload: Uses highly optimized Google Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) payload format to reduce traffic.
State Management: Integrates birth/death certificate sequences to track online states of industrial PLCs.

slides.title: Knowledge Checks
slides.subtitle: Test your understanding of MQTT core concepts, wildcards, and features

slides.title: QoS Handshake Check
slides.question: Which MQTT QoS level implements a four-way handshake to guarantee that a message is delivered exactly once?
slides.explanation: QoS 2 uses a four-way handshake (PUBLISH -> PUBREC -> PUBREL -> PUBCOMP) to guarantee that the message is delivered exactly once and duplicate deliveries are prevented.

slides.title: Wildcard Matching Logic
slides.question: Given the subscription topic filter 'home/+/status', which of the following topic paths will NOT match?
slides.explanation: The '+' wildcard matches exactly one level. The path 'home/livingroom/lights/status' has four levels and fails to match. It would require 'home/+/+/status' or 'home/#'.

slides.title: MQTT v5.0 Shared Subscriptions
slides.question: What is the primary benefit of MQTT v5.0 Shared Subscriptions?
options: They allow messages to be shared across different internet protocols.
They load-balance messages round-robin among a group of subscribing consumers.
They force brokers to store all published messages permanently.
They encrypt subscriptions to hide subscriber identities.
slides.answer: They load-balance messages round-robin among a group of subscribing consumers.
slides.explanation: Shared Subscriptions allow a pool of client consumers to share the message workload of a single subscription, distributing publishes round-robin.

slides.title: Last Will and Testament (LWT)
slides.question: When does the broker publish a client's Last Will and Testament (LWT) message?
options: When the client disconnects cleanly by sending a DISCONNECT packet.
When the client successfully establishes a new connection.
Only when the client disconnects ungracefully due to network or power loss.
Every time the client completes a QoS 2 handshake.
slides.answer: Only when the client disconnects ungracefully due to network or power loss.
slides.explanation: LWT messages are only sent if the client connection is closed ungracefully without sending a DISCONNECT packet (e.g. heartbeat timeout or TCP link loss).

slides.title: Conclusion & Resources
slides.subtitle: Wrapping up and next steps in your IoT journey

list.text: Lightweight & Robust: MQTT is the backbone of IoT, offering minimal overhead and high resiliency for unstable networks.
MQTT v5.0 Ready: Leverages modern features like user properties, shared subscriptions, and session expiry for enterprise scalability.
Security First: Hardens production systems using TLS encryption, client certificate authentication (mTLS), and strict topic ACLs.
MQTT Explorer: A desktop client that visualizes topic structures in an intuitive, hierarchical tree view.
CLI Tools: Using 'mosquitto_pub' and 'mosquitto_sub' command-line utilities for rapid script testing.
Wireshark: Analyzing packet structures and decoding unencrypted MQTT frames for deep network troubleshooting.

slides.title: Thank You!
slides.message: Start building your next smart device with MQTT! Q&A Session.
